summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the state of solid state drives a year after his previous coverage, arguing that SSDs remain the single best performance upgrade for any computer despite modest price declines. He notes that other PC components like CPUs and RAM have become commoditized and boring, while SSDs still offer a transformative experience. The post also highlights the emergence of hybrid drives like Seagate's Momentus XT, which combine flash memory with traditional platters to offer a compelling middle ground between pure SSDs and spinning disks. Atwood recommends hybrid drives for laptops and secondary machines where full SSD investment isn't justified.
key_points: SSDs remain the most impactful performance upgrade you can make, offering a 'life changing experience' that other components can't match
128GB has become the practical minimum SSD size, declining from $350 to $250, while 64GB requires too much disk space management
CPUs and RAM have become commoditized - they're all 'stupid fast' and cheap, making them boring upgrades
The Crucial RealSSD C300 is the current performance leader, though OS support for TRIM is critical to avoid degradation
Hybrid drives like Seagate's Momentus XT finally deliver on the 2006 promise of combining flash speed with platter capacity at reasonable prices
money_quotes: Solid state hard drives remain a life changing experience.
A solid state hard drive is easily the best and most obvious performance upgrade you can make on any computer for a given amount of money.
CPUs? All stupid fast at any price, with more cores and gigahertz than you'll ever need unless you're one of those freaks who does nothing but raytracing and video transcoding all day long.
I spend almost zero real world time waiting for the CPU to do anything on my computer.
Skip over the old and busted spinning rust hard drives, and choose a newfangled solid state hard drive.
Like all truly sexy things, what turns me on is the part I can't see – the sexy, sexy, sexy performance inside this baby.
A hundred bucks for near-SSD performance and decent capacity? Sign me up.
key_insight: While most PC components have become commoditized to the point of irrelevance, storage remains the one area where spending more money delivers a genuinely transformative performance improvement.
